summary: I was determined to get into Officers' Candidate School despite initial setbacks, like failing the eye exam. I memorized eye charts to pass it eventually, and after passing my physical at Camp Fannin, I attended OCS at Camp Lee, Virginia. After about three months, I graduated as a second lieutenant in the quartermaster corps, which set the course for my military career during World War II.

Camp Fannin was where I took my physical exam before entering Officers' Candidate School. It was a crucial step in my journey to becoming an officer during World War II. Passing the physical there allowed me to move on to OCS at Camp Lee and eventually serve in the Philippines.

After passing my physical at Camp Fannin, I went to Officers' Candidate School at Camp Lee, Virginia. I spent about three months there training and came out as a second lieutenant in the quartermaster corps. Camp Lee was where I transitioned from enlisted to officer during the height of World War II.

I was sent to the Philippines during World War II after completing OCS. When we landed, I was given command of an infantry platoon despite being in the quartermaster corps. I spent about a year there, involved in combat near Manila and later in civil affairs work, helping restore civilian government after the Japanese occupation.

The Japanese had occupied the Philippines for most of World War II before we arrived. When I was in the Philippine Civil Affairs Unit, our job was to restore civilian government after this long occupation. It was a challenging time, dealing with the aftermath of years under Japanese control and helping free POWs around Manila.

In Manila, I was part of a cleaning-up operation in the suburbs, facing resistance and sporadic gunfire from Japanese soldiers. Later, as part of the Philippine Civil Affairs Unit, I was placed in charge of an entire district of the city, running schools and local governance. It was a complex role, especially when I discovered a major house of prostitution operating in a school under my watch, which I had to shut down.

I was selected to join the Philippine Civil Affairs Unit during the liberation of the Philippines in World War II. Our mission was to restore civilian government and transfer authority back to the Filipino people after the Japanese occupation. I spent most of my time in Manila with the PCAU, freeing POWs and managing a city district as a military governor.

Shortly before the end of World War II, I was sent to a rest camp in Baguio, northern Luzon. Since I was the only quartermaster officer there, I was put in charge of the kitchen, even though I had no experience. It was there that the Japanese surrender was announced, and soldiers left the camp to celebrate in Manila, which was quite a strange sight.

I spent time in northern Luzon at a rest camp in Baguio near the end of World War II. Luzon was a significant area during the war, and after the Japanese surrender, many troops left the camp to celebrate in Manila. My role there was limited but important, as I managed the kitchen despite having no prior experience.

My entire military service, from Officers' Candidate School through my time in the Philippines and Korea, took place during World War II. I saw combat, helped restore civilian government after Japanese occupations, and experienced the challenges of military life. The war taught me discipline, endurance, and how to handle authority and diverse people, shaping me into a more mature person by the time I was discharged in 1946.

Japan was the occupying force in both the Philippines and Korea during World War II. After Japan surrendered, we were initially scheduled to go there for an invasion that never happened. Instead, we were redirected to Korea to help set up a transition government after the Japanese left, marking a significant change in the region's control.

After World War II ended, I was sent to Korea, specifically Seoul, to help establish a transition government following the Japanese occupation. The Koreans were unprepared for independence after forty years of Japanese control, so our unit stepped in to manage the situation. I performed a role similar to what I had done in Manila until my discharge in the summer of 1946.

In Seoul, Korea, I helped set up a transition government after the Japanese occupation ended. It was a challenging time because the Koreans were not ready for independence after forty years under Japanese rule. My experience in Manila helped me manage similar responsibilities there until I was discharged in 1946.

The Japanese occupied Korea for forty years before the end of World War II, controlling everything in the country. When Japan surrendered, the Koreans were left unprepared for independence, so our unit moved in to establish a transition government. I was part of that effort in Seoul, helping to stabilize the situation until my discharge in 1946.
